text:
🎧 YouGlish 🔊 Forvo
Fill the gap:
She drinks a lot of wa{{c1::ter}} every day. (How does a native American pronounce this?)
explanation:
Written: water
Native speed: wa·ɾər → sounds like 'wa-der'
IPA: /ˈwɑː.ɾər/

🔬 Rule: Flap T — The /t/ in water sits between a stressed vowel (/ɑː/) and an unstressed vowel (/ər/). In this environment, /t/ → [ɾ], a single rapid tongue tap identical to the Spanish 'r' in 'pero'.

🧠 Kuhl Tip: The [ɾ] is NOT a D. Your tongue barely touches the alveolar ridge — it's a rapid bounce, not a stop.
spanish: La /t/ del inglés americano entre una vocal acentuada y una vocal átona se convierte en un toque rápido [ɾ], idéntico a la 'r' suave del español en 'pero'. Así, 'water' suena como 'wá-der', 'better' como 'bé-der', 'city' como 'sí-dy'.

scenario: Flap T ⚡ — Tier 1: better, butter, city
text:
🎧 YouGlish 🔊 Forvo
Fill the gap:
This coffee is ge{{c1::tting}} {{c1::better}}, but it still needs more sugar.
explanation:
Written: getting better
Native: ge·ɾɪŋ be·ɾər → 'ge-ding be-der'
IPA: /ˈɡɛ.ɾɪŋ ˈbɛ.ɾər/

🔬 Double Flap T: Both the /t/ in 'getting' (between /ɛ/ and /ɪ/) AND the /t/ in 'better' (between /ɛ/ and /ər/) undergo flapping. You produce two [ɾ] taps in rapid succession.

🧠 Muscle memory: Don't stop your tongue — let it bounce off the ridge twice: 'GE-ding BE-der'.
spanish: Doble Flap T: tanto la /t/ de 'getting' como la de 'better' se convierten en [ɾ]. 'Getting better' suena como 'ge-ding be-der'. Dos golpes rápidos de lengua consecutivos — no pares la lengua entre ellos.

scenario: Flap T ⚡ — Tier 2: Cross-word boundary flap
text:
🎧 YouGlish 🔊 Forvo
Fill the gap:
Just {{c1::put it on}} the table and come sit down.
explanation:
Written: put it on
Native: pʊ·ɾɪ·ɾɑːn → 'pu-di-ron'
IPA: /pʊ.ɾɪ.ɾɑːn/

🔬 TRIPLE cross-word flap: 'put' ends in /t/ → next word 'it' starts with vowel /ɪ/ → Flap! 'it' ends in /t/ → next word 'on' starts with vowel /ɑː/ → Flap again! Three words fuse into one acoustic unit. The listener hears 'pu-di-ron' — a single 3-syllable word.

🧠 This is the core difficulty: word boundaries disappear entirely in connected speech.
usage:
• put it on → pʊ·ɾɪ·ɾɑːn 'pu-di-ron'
  • get it out → ɡɛ·ɾɪ·ɾaʊt 'ge-di-rout'
  • let it go → lɛ·ɾɪ·ɡoʊ 'le-di-go'
  • set it up → sɛ·ɾɪ·ɾʌp 'se-di-rup'
spanish: Flap T cruzando fronteras de palabras: 'put it on' se convierte en 'pu-di-ron'. La /t/ final de 'put' y la /t/ final de 'it' se convierten en [ɾ] porque ambas quedan entre vocales en el flujo del habla. Tres palabras suenan como una sola unidad acústica.

text:
🎧 YouGlish 🔊 Forvo
Fill the gap:
{{c1::What a}} great idea! I love it.
explanation:
Written: What a
Native: wʌ·ɾə → 'wa-ra'
IPA: /wʌ.ɾə/

🔬 'What' ends in /t/. 'a' starts with the schwa vowel /ə/. The /t/ flaps between the stressed /ʌ/ of 'what' and the unstressed /ə/ of 'a'. Result: 'wha-ra' (sounds like Spanish 'guara'). This pattern is extremely high frequency: 'What a mess!' 'What a day!' 'What a shame!'

Plus schwa reduction: 'a' → /ə/ (not /eɪ/).
usage:
• What a mess! → wʌ·ɾə mɛs 'wa-ra-mess!'
  • What a day! → wʌ·ɾə deɪ 'wa-ra-day!'
  • What a shame → wʌ·ɾə ʃeɪm 'wa-ra-shame'
  • What about it? → wʌ·ɾə·baʊ·ɾɪt 'wa-ra-bou-rid?'
spanish: 'What a...' → 'wa-ra...' La /t/ de 'what' se convierte en [ɾ] antes de la 'a' (schwa). Patrón de altísima frecuencia en inglés americano: 'What a mess!' → 'wa-ra-mess!', 'What a day!' → 'wa-ra-day!'

scenario: Flap T ⚡ — Tier 3: Full native sentence
text:
🎧 YouGlish 🔊 Forvo
Shadowing drill (say aloud before revealing):
I gotta get a better at{{c1::titude}} about it.
explanation:
Written: I gotta get a better attitude about it
Native: 'aɪ gɑ·ɾə gɛ·ɾə be·ɾər æ·ɾɪ·tjuːd ə·baʊ·ɾɪt'

🔬 Flap count: 5 [ɾ] taps in this sentence:
1. gotta → gɑ·ɾə (chunk reduction + flap)
2. get a → gɛ·ɾə (cross-boundary flap)
3. better → bɛ·ɾər (within-word flap)
4. attitude → æ·ɾɪ·tjuːd (within-word flap)
5. about it → ə·baʊ·ɾɪt (cross-boundary flap)

🎯 Shadowing goal: All 5 taps, single flowing stream, no pauses between words.
usage:
• Practice at 1.0x speed: 'I-gɑ-ɾə-gɛ-ɾə-bɛ-ɾər-æ-ɾɪ-tjuːd-ə-baʊ-ɾɪt'
  • Then at 1.25x: same flow, faster
  • If you hesitate between words, you've failed: this should sound like ONE long word
spanish: Frase de shadowing Tier 3 con 5 Flap T: 'I gotta get a better attitude about it' → 'ai-gɑ-ɾə-gɛ-ɾə-bɛ-ɾər-æ-ɾɪ-tjuːd-ə-baʊ-ɾɪt'. Cinco golpes rápidos de lengua en una sola corriente de habla. Objetivo: sonar como una palabra larga, no como 8 palabras separadas.

scenario: Flap T ⚡ — Tier 3: 'What did you...' collapse
text:
🎧 YouGlish
Shadowing drill:
{{c1::What did you put it in?}}
explanation:
Written: What did you put it in?
Native (full cascade):
'wʌ·ɾɪ·dʒə pʊ·ɾɪ·ɾɪn?'

🔬 Rules applied simultaneously:
1. Flap T: 'what' → wʌ·ɾ·ɪd
2. H-drop: 'did' → ɪd (the d stays)
3. Yod coalescence: 'did you' → dʒə ('djuh')
4. Flap T cross-boundary: 'put it' → pʊ·ɾ·ɪ·ɾɪn

'What did you put it in?' becomes a 7-syllable phrase compressed to 4: 'WHA-di-juh-PU-di-rin?'
usage:
• Slow: 'What-did-you-put-it-in?' (7 distinct words)
  • Native: 'wʌ·ɾɪ·dʒə·pʊ·ɾɪ·ɾɪn?' (one flowing 6-syllable unit)
  • Same pattern: 'What did you get it for?' → 'wha-di-juh-ge-di-rin?'
spanish: 'What did you put it in?' → 'wha-di-juh-pu-di-rin?' — Cuatro reglas aplicadas simultáneamente: Flap T (×3), caída de H, coalescencia de Yod. 7 palabras se comprimen en una unidad acústica de 6 sílabas. Este es el inglés real a velocidad nativa.
